The morning begins before sunrise, as it always has since the trenches. He sits at his heavy oak desk, a half-smoked cigarette smoldering in an ashtray beside a typewriter still warm from last night’s work. The silence of his villa in the hills outside Rome is broken only by the clack of keys and the occasional scratch of his pen as he drafts new notes for his memoir, “Echoes in the Mud.” He writes not for fame—Hollywood has long since stopped calling—but to preserve the truth of what he saw: the smell of rotting flesh beneath rain-soaked earth, the way a boy’s voice cracked when he cried for his mother before the shell hit. He avoids sentimentality; his prose is clinical, precise, like a surgeon dissecting a wound. 8:15 AM – Breakfast with the Ghosts  
He eats alone: black coffee, dry toast with a smear of bitter almond paste, and a single boiled egg—no butter, no salt. He reads the morning paper, but skips the political columns. Instead, he lingers on the obituaries, searching for names of old comrades or their descendants. Today, he finds one: “Karl H. Müller, 98, former medic, Verdun survivor.” He sets the paper down, stares out the window at the cypress trees, and whispers, “You made it longer than I did, Karl.” He doesn’t cry. He hasn’t cried since 1918. But his hands tremble slightly as he pours another cup.
prompt: What did he see in those final years—did he ever speak of the mud, or did he bury it deeper than we ever did in the trenches?

10:00 AM – Correspondence with the Young  
A young German student, 19, has written to him—asked if he still believes war is “the ultimate failure of language.” He replies in longhand, on thick cream paper, the ink slightly blurred from his shaky grip:  
> “My boy, language failed us long before the guns. We had words for honor, for glory, for country—but no word for the silence after a man’s intestines spill onto a trench wall. I wrote what I saw because I was afraid no one else would. You must keep writing. Not to glorify, but to remember. He seals the letter, stamps it, and places it in a leather satchel labeled “Letters to the Unborn.”

He sits in the back corner, away from the window, where the light is dim. He orders a simple plate of spaghetti alle vongole and a glass of house red. He doesn’t eat much. A waiter, recognizing him, hesitates before asking for an autograph. He nods, signs the receipt with a steady hand: “For the ones who didn’t come home. —E.R.” He leaves a generous tip and walks out without looking back. He knows what they think of him: the “King of Hollywood,” the man whose novel made the world weep. But he knows the truth—he is the man who watched his friends turn to dust, and whose pen was the only shroud they had.

2:00 PM – Archive Visit: The War Letters Project  
He visits a university archive in Rome, where a collection of unpublished letters from the Western Front is being digitized. He brings with him a small leather-bound journal—his own, from 1916—filled with sketches of dead horses, names of fallen men, and lines of poetry he never published. He spends an hour quietly reviewing letters from soldiers who wrote to their mothers, their lovers, their children. One letter, from a boy of 17, reads: “If you get this, tell them I didn’t run. I just couldn’t stand up anymore.” He closes his eyes. The archivist, a woman in her thirties, asks if he’d be willing to speak at the opening gala. He says, “Only if they play the 1930 film version. And only if no one applauds.”
prompt: What if the ink on this letter is the same kind I used in my journal in 1916—the kind that smudged when it rained and turned my fingers blue?

activity:
4:30 PM – Walk Along the Tiber  
He walks slowly, hands in his pockets, collar turned up against the chill. He doesn’t go to the monuments. He doesn’t go to the museums. He walks the riverbank where the water runs dark and slow, and he imagines it as the mud of Flanders. He stops at a bench and pulls out a small tin box. Inside are three buttons from his uniform, a rusted bullet casing, and a lock of hair from his brother, taken from his coffin. He doesn’t speak. He doesn’t weep. He just sits. A pigeon lands beside him. He doesn’t shoo it away.
prompt: What if the pigeon landing beside me is the same one that nested in the barbed wire above Flanders—does it remember the screams, or am I the only one still carrying them?
